course_title: Applied Research in Criminology and Criminal Justice 1
course_description:
Research and project planning skills are highly valued by employers and are widely used in academic, government and non-governmental agencies, private companies and community organisations. This course aims to enhance the skills and knowledge of theory and research methods developed in other degree courses through an applied group research project in the field of crime and justice. Students will develop their research skills in response to current issues and build confidence in delivering impactful outputs that are valued by industry. Students become skilled in project planning, team work, and industry liaison.

This course is only available to BCCJ students and is recommended to be completed in the final year of study.

Welcome to CRIM3201. I trust that you will enjoy this course, and its companion - CRIM3202 in Semester 2. These courses will be some of the most exciting, relevant, and challenging learningsﾠthat you are likely to undertake in your entire degree. This is because the courses are a 'capstone' experience that gives you the opportunity to use and develop applied research skills and knowledge that you have gained in earlier criminology courses to a more advanced level. This occurs through the design and execution of a 'real world' applied research project working with an industry partner from the government, community, or corporate sector. Emphasis is placed on the development of skills in critically evaluating research; planning a researchﾠproject;ﾠwriting a research proposal; working as part of a research team; generating and analysing research data; and reporting to various audiences.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
We recommend completing the following courses before enrolling in this one:
companions: We recommend completing the following courses at the same time:
restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BCCJ and BCCJ/BA

course_contacts.name: Student Enquiries School of Social Science

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
The course is formally structured around weekly 3 hour interactive workshops where students will primarily work in small research groups.
In addition to workshops, student research teams will need to regularly meet to undertake the course learning and assessment requirements.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Undertake necessary preparation for applied research projects, including liaising with research and industry partners and/or clients
Construct a critical review of a published body of research in your discipline area
Construct a research proposal using a recognised research design methodology within the Criminology discipline
Engage actively and effectively in teamwork
Report on research work in progress to various audiences
Relate you learning to the practice of criminology and how your work is distinguished from existing knowledge, as well as articulating the significance of the research.
Apply an understanding of the ethical implications of research

assessment_summary.title: Weekly Project Planning Tasks
assessment_summary.due_date: 3/03/2025 - 19/05/2025; Tasks are completed during class in Weeks 2-12, excluding week 3 due to TC Alfred.

assessment_details.title: Weekly Project Planning Tasks
assessment_details.due_date: 3/03/2025 - 19/05/2025 Tasks are completed during class in Weeks 2-12, excluding week 3 due to TC Alfred.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: For this task, you will be asked to collaborate with your group members via Microsoft Teams, a widely used collaborative tool in Industry. In our workshop each week, you will work together on various aspects of project planning to progress and develop your ideas collaboratively. Each week, by the end of the workshop, specific tasks will need to be recorded on your groups Teams page. Your contribution to the weekly planning will be assessed both during your in-class discussions, and through the contributions you make on Teams during the session. If you are unable to attend a workshop due to illness/other unforeseen circumstances, please contact the course coordinator to determine how best to contribute to your team’s project planning for that week. Artificial Intelligence (AI) provides emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI in each instance and be clear why they have chosen to use AI. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Tasks will be assessed within the Microsoft Teams group page for each project group.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
As the tasks are to be completed with the project group, extensions are not permitted. If a student is unwell/unable to attend class due to other unforeseen circumstances, please contact the course coordinator prior to class to determine how best to contribute to your team’s project planning for that week.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
As the tasks are to be completed with the project group; late submissions will not be awarded any marks.

assessment_details.title: Applied Annotated Bibliography
assessment_details.task_description: In this individual piece of assessment, you are required to identify 5 pieces of literature for the development and planning of your research proposal. You must provide a brief statement summarising your research problem, provide a summary of the key findings/argument of each piece; and explain how each piece is relevant to your proposed research. This can include articles relevant to your methodology, such a study using similar methodology, not necessarily on the same substantive topic. There is no set word limit on each item but the overall length of your Annotated Bibliography assignment should not exceed 1400 words, excluding the bibliographic details of key references and your full reference list for the assignment. You can work as a team in formulating your research problem or devise your own. If you all use the same research problem, you will all receive the same mark for that component of the assessment. Writing separate ones may actually benefit you as a team since you'll end up with multiple iterations of your research problem, allowing you to make comparisons on how you each perceive the project and to select the most suitable for inclusion in your research proposal further down the track. Research teams must work together to plan the focus of work for each team member so that, taken together, the references submitted by each student and the feedback they receive will assist in your team's review of literature for your Final Research Proposal. In compiling your key references, you should use APA style referencing. Use of a referencing tool (e.g. Zotero) is highly encouraged. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Students must complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assignment. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your assignment electronically by the due time, on the due date.
Your assignment must be submitted via Turnitin on blackboard. To submit your assignment electronically log in to https://learn.uq.edu.au/ultra with your UQ username and password, then click on Course Code>>Assessment>>Assignments, and use the appropriate assignment submission link for each piece of assessment. No e-mailed submissions of assessments will be accepted.
By uploading your assignment via Turnitin, you are certifying that the work you submit is your own work except where correctly attributed to another source. Do not submit your assignment if it contains any work that is not your own.
You are required to retain proof of submission of your assessment. Your Digital Receipt is available for download from your Assignment Dashboard. If you cannot see your submission and download your digital receipt, your assessment has not been successfully submitted; please submit again.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties with Blackboard, please contact the ITS Support Team .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
You must submit a request for an extension as soon as it becomes clear you need an extension. Your request should be submitted no later than the assessment item's due date and time.
The request must be accompanied by supporting documentation corroborating the reason for the request. A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
In the School of Social Science, extensions on the basis of an approved Student Access Plan (SAP) or an Extension Verification Letter (EVL) can be approved for a maximum period of 7 calendar days. Subsequent extensions for a piece of assessment will require additional supporting documentation (e.g., a medical certificate or other supporting evidence listed on my.UQ ).
Extension requests exceeding the maximum extension period stated for a piece of assessment will only be considered under exceptional circumstances (circumstances outside of your control) with additional supporting documentation.
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Students may be asked to supply the work they have completed to date on the assessment piece. This is to establish what efforts have already been made to complete the assessment, and whether the proposed work plan is feasible. Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation by up to one semester.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Work will not be accepted if it is more than one week (7 calendar days) late without prior approval.

assessment_details.title: Ethics Application Task
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: A core component of social research involves considering the ethical implications of our actions (or inactions). Before embarking on your research, ethical clearance from an UQ Ethics Committee is likely to be needed. This process requires weighing the risks and benefits of the research and any distress or discomfort that may be caused by what you are doing, and provide the Ethics Committee a clear description of your project and how your will manage these risks and consent of research participants. Each project will have different ethical considerations. In order to give you some practice in this process for your project and projects you may complete in the future, you will need to consider two of the three scenarios and describe (in ~500 words each) any (a) potential risks/harms relating to human participants, (b) how you might manage those risks, and (c) your responsibilities in managing the data. Resources will be provided in class, but the National Statement on Ethical Conduct in Human Research will be fundamental to your task: ( https://www.nhmrc.gov.au/about-us/publications/national-statement-ethical-conduct-human-research-2023 ) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of an assessment task about ETHICAL decisions and considerations will require students to critically engage with the specific contexts presented in an ETHICAL manner, for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. If AI is utilised in this assessment task, it must be referenced and clearly explained why its use was necessary. Students must complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assignment. Failure to do so will result in a grade of zero for the assignment. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Proposal Poster Presentation and Plan
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Being able to summarise and present your work concisely or to a varied audience is an important skill. For this piece of assessment groups will need to represent their proposed project design in a visually engaging poster accompanied by a verbal (brief presentation ~5-10mins) and full written proposal. Presentation Students’ explanations must cover the elements represented in the poster (research problem/gap/issue, research questions/aims, proposed methods and framework). Students will deliver their presentations during class in Week 13, and their poster and will be submitted via Blackboard on that day, along with their full proposal (detailed below). Presentations will be recorded and stored via Echo360 for marking purposes. Posters should be designed in a landscape orientation and should ensure all fonts are of a readable size. Abstracts can be single or 1.5 spaced and should have normal margins. Proposal In this piece of assessment, you are required to submit a proposal outlining your research problem and your plan for undertaking the research. In your proposal, you should outline the following: · a clear problem statement that the project seeks to address; · rationale (reason) for your research, including its contribution to the broad field of practice and scholarly literature in which the work sits, plus any anticipated outcomes; · the specific aims of your proposed research and a statement of your research questions; · any concepts or theories that you draw on; · the methods you propose to use; · how you will sample and recruit participants and/or obtain documents or other data for your research, and gather other information; · how you will record and manage data; · how you will conduct your analysis; · a timeline and budget of required resources. · any ethical concerns arising from your project and how you propose to address them. You will be provided with critical feedback based on the key criteria for assessment of your Group Research Project Proposal, which will be useful when embarking upon your research in CRIM3202. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Students must complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assignment. Students must also complete a Buddycheck, assessing the contributions of others in their project team. Failure to include the AI declaration or complete the Buddycheck will result in a grade of zero for the assignment. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may also constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Course aims and the learning environment NOTE: The available research projects will be presented in Week 1. You will nominate you project preference and write an Expression of Interest. This should be submitted via Blackboard by 5pm on Wednesday 26 February. Learning outcomes: L01
Week 2: Learning to work as a research team Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Week 3 cancelled due to TC Alfred Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05, L08
Week 4: Getting Started Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L07
Week 5: Developing your research project Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L07
Week 6: Operationalising your research plan Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L07
Week 7: Sharing your Work in Progress Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
Week 8: Ethics Applications Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L06, L08
Week 9: Managing Group Dynamics Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07

Week 10: Labour Day Public Holiday - No workshop Note, there will be catch-up weekly project planning tasks in this week that will still need to be completed despite no class Learning outcomes: L02, L05
Week 11: Preparing for data collection Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L07, L08
Week 12: Consolidating your research proposal Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
Week 13: Proposal Poster Presentations Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
